docs.title: Proton
docs.text:
A flexible and powerful UI library that allows you to create instances and give them functionality easily, like this:

local SGui = new "ScreenGui" {
["Name"] = "My Gui",
["Parent"] = LocalPlayer.PlayerGui,
["Children"] = {
new "TextButton" {
["Size"] = UDim2.new(0, 250, 0, 100),
["Text"] = "Click me to do something cool",
["*MouseButton1Click"] = (function()
print("Left mouse button clicked")
end)
}
}
}

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
The syntax basically boils down to this.

local Instance = new "ClassName" { Properties }

What sets Proton apart from other UI libraries is that you can use the Properties table to not only set properties but to connect events, parent instances, and more.

To connect an event, append an asterisk to the event name, like so:

new "ClassName" {
["*EventName"] = (function)
}

Furthermore, use Special Properties to easily, nonverbosely add functionality to your instances. For example, use the Children Special Property to parents the given instance(s) to the new instance:

new "ClassName" {
["Children"] = {
Instances
}
}

For more information, see Special Properties.

docs.title: Connect event to instance
docs.text:
When creating a new instance with new() you can easily connect an event to it by simply adding it to the property table, appended with an asterisk, like such:

new "ClassName" {
["*EventName"] = func
}

new "TextButton" {
["*MouseButton1Click"] = (function()
print("Left mouse button clicked")
end)
}

Arguments will still function as usual:

new "Part" {
["*Touched"] = (function(object)
print("Part was touched by", object:GetFullName())
end)
}

If you want to connect multiple functions to a single event, use the "Events" Special Property instead.

new "TextButton" {
["Name"] = "My Text Button",
["Parent"] = ScreenGui,
["*MouseButton1Click"] = (function()
print("Left mouse button clicked")
end),
["Events"] = {
{ "MouseButton1Click", (function()
print("Left mouse button clicked")
end) },
{ "MouseButton2Down", (function()
print("Right mouse button down")
end) }
}
}

docs.title: Special Properties
docs.text: Special Properties are used when creating a function with new() and allow you to easily, nonverbosely add functionality to your instances.

docs.title: Children
docs.text:
Parents given instance(s) to the new instance.

new "ClassName" {
["Children"] = { Instances }
}

new "ScreenGui" {
["Children"] = {
ExistingInstance,
new("Frame"){}
}
}

docs.title: Events
docs.text:
Connects events to the new instance.

new "ClassName" {
["Events"] = {
{EventName, Function},
... 
}
}

new "TextButton" {
["Events"] = {
{ "MouseButton1Click", (function() print("Left mouse button clicked") end) },
{ "MouseButton1Click", (function() print("This function runs at the same time as the above one") end) },
{ "MouseButton2Down", (function() print("Right mouse button down") end) }
}
}

docs.title: Compile Proton
docs.text: Download a precompiled build here or build one yourself.

docs.title: Use a precompiled build
docs.text:
Download a precompiled build here. You only need to download the .rbxm file; import this into Studio by dragging and dropping the file onto the Studio window.

A ModuleScript named Proton will be imported. You can parent it anywhere, but we suggest ReplicatedStorage.

docs.title: Build it yourself
docs.text:
• Create a ModuleScript named Proton. Parent it anywhere, we suggest ReplicatedStorage
  • Paste in the contents of main.lua
  • Create a folder named functions; parent it to the ModuleScript
  • For each file in the src/functions directory of Proton's source code, create a new ModuleScript in the functions folder and paste in the contents of the file. Rename the ModuleScript to the file's name, omitting the file extension.
  • Done

docs.title: Import Proton
docs.text:
Import Proton to your script using require(), then import the new function with Proton.require(). You can use any name for the Proton and new variables if you wish.

Warning

Please keep in mind that Lua's built-in require function is different from the Proton.require function.

local Proton = require(--[[Proton script location]])
local new = Proton.require("new")

docs.title: Verbose Mode
docs.text:
Forces Proton to print extra debug information that is not necessary for normal use but may be required in certain debugging scenarios. Verbose Mode can be enabled or disabled at any time. By default, it is disabled.

To enable Verbose Mode, insert this line in any script.

Proton.verbose(true)

Alternatively, you can set local verbose = true in main.lua. This is recommended if you don't want to disable it later.

Likewise, if you want to disable Verbose Mode later, call this code:

Proton.verbose(false)

To check for Verbose Mode, call this function:

Proton.verboseEnabled()
